name: Wallis and Futuna (Wallis-et-Futuna)
summary: Wallis and Futuna is a Pacific Island civil-law overseas collectivity (Collectivité d'outre-mer) of the French Republic — structurally distinctive globally as the only state-level entity with three constitutionally-recognised indigenous kingdoms (Uvea, Sigave, Alo) operating in parallel with the French Republic civil-law framework. The Statute Law 61-814 of 29 July 1961 recognises the three traditional kingdoms and their kings (Lavelua of Uvea, Tu'i Sigave of Sigave, Tu'i Agaifo of Alo) within the French Republic constitutional framework. Family-law framework operates under the French Civil Code (Code Civil) for civil-status (statut civil de droit commun) matters and under Wallisian/Futunan customary-law for customary-status (statut civil coutumier) matters via the customary courts of the three kingdoms. Parental authority and child custody operate under Civil Code arts. 371-387 (civil-status) or under indigenous customary-law (customary-status). The Court of Appeal of Nouméa is the apex domestic appellate court for civil and criminal matters in Wallis and Futuna; final appellate jurisdiction was retained with the French Court of Cassation in Paris. Family-law matters are heard at first instance in the Tribunal of First Instance of Mata-Utu and on customary-status matters via Customary Sessions before the three kings. Psychology profession is regulated through the French Republic professional framework as applicable in Wallis and Futuna. Wallis and Futuna is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label; courts operate substantively under the interest-of-the-child standard. Wallis and Futuna is a Hague Convention 1980 party via French Republic territorial extension effective 1 December 1983.

statutory_framework.title: Statute of Wallis and Futuna
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal Statute Law establishing Wallis and Futuna overseas collectivity status and constitutionally-recognising the three traditional kingdoms within the French Republic framework.

statutory_framework.citation: French Civil Code arts. 371-387 (applicable in Wallis and Futuna)
statutory_framework.title: French Civil Code — Parental authority
statutory_framework.relevance: French Civil Code applicable to civil-status (statut civil de droit commun) persons in Wallis and Futuna. Arts. 371-387 govern autorité parentale and child custody.

statutory_framework.title: Wallisian/Futunan customary-status framework
statutory_framework.relevance: Constitutionally-recognised customary-status (statut civil coutumier) for indigenous Wallisian/Futunan persons operating via the customary courts of the three kingdoms.

apex_courts.name: Court of Appeal of Nouméa (Cour d'appel de Nouméa)
apex_courts.role: Apex domestic appellate court for civil and criminal matters from Wallis and Futuna.

apex_courts.name: French Court of Cassation (Cour de Cassation)
apex_courts.role: Final appellate court for civil and criminal matters from Wallis and Futuna.

professional_regulators.name: French Republic professional framework (applicable in Wallis and Futuna)
professional_regulators.role: French professional regulatory framework for health and allied health professionals including clinical psychology applicable in Wallis and Futuna.

anonymisation_convention: Wallisian/Futunan family-court decisions are anonymised per Court of Appeal of Nouméa practice using initials.

key_developments.title: French protectorate establishment + Lavelua Amelia + colonial-era kingdoms framework
key_developments.description: French protectorate over Wallis (Uvea) established 5 April 1842 — Lavelua Amelia Tokagahahau Fenuamoana (queen of Uvea) requested French protection. Substantively distinctive Pacific Catholic-mission-and-monarchy-based protectorate framework. Subsequent Futuna protectorate 1888. Foundational three-kingdoms framework affecting subsequent constitutional-administrative-trajectory.

key_developments.title: Futuna protectorate + Sigave + Alo kingdoms framework consolidation
key_developments.description: Futuna protectorate established 1888 with Tu'i Sigave (king of Sigave) and Tu'i Agaifo (king of Alo) — consolidating three-kingdoms framework. Substantive Pacific Catholic-Marist-mission-influenced administrative framework establishing foundational basis for subsequent constitutional-recognition trajectory.

key_developments.title: Overseas territory status 1946 + post-WWII framework
key_developments.description: Wallis and Futuna became Overseas Territory (Territoire d'outre-mer) of French Republic 1946 within French Union framework. Substantive constitutional-restructuring affecting post-WWII Pacific French territorial-administration framework with continuing three-kingdoms recognition.

key_developments.title: Referendum 1959 + transition-to-Statute-Law-1961 trajectory
key_developments.description: Wallis and Futuna 1959 referendum substantively determining transition trajectory — 94% in favour of Territoire d'outre-mer status with constitutionally-recognised three-kingdoms framework. Foundational referendum framework for subsequent 1961 Statute Law.

key_developments.title: Statute Law 61-814 — overseas collectivity + three kingdoms recognition
key_developments.description: Federal Statute Law 29 July 1961 establishing Wallis and Futuna overseas collectivity status and constitutionally-recognising the three traditional kingdoms (Uvea, Sigave, Alo) within the French Republic framework. Substantively distinctive globally: only state-level entity with three constitutionally-recognised indigenous kingdoms operating in parallel with civil-law framework.

key_developments.title: Hague Convention 1980 territorial extension
key_developments.description: Hague Convention 1980 territorial extension by France to Wallis and Futuna effective 1 December 1983 — establishing Pacific Hague Convention jurisdiction framework within French Republic territorial framework.

key_developments.title: France ratifies UN Convention on the Rights of the Child + Wallis and Futuna extension
key_developments.description: France ratified the UNCRC on 7 August 1990 with extension to Wallis and Futuna — establishing best-interests-of-the-child substantive doctrine within French-civil-law overseas-collectivity framework with customary-status accommodation.

key_developments.title: Constitutional reform 2003 + collectivité d'outre-mer recategorisation + Article 74
key_developments.description: Constitutional reform 28 March 2003 substantively recategorising Wallis and Futuna as Collectivité d'outre-mer (Article 74) — substantively reforming political-institutional framework while preserving three-kingdoms constitutional-recognition framework. Substantive constitutional anchor for contemporary family-law jurisprudence.

key_developments.title: Lavelua succession crisis + three-kingdoms governance-framework contestation
key_developments.description: Wallisian Lavelua (king of Uvea) succession crisis 2014-2018 substantively affecting traditional-governance framework within Wallis. Substantively distinctive Pacific Catholic-monarchy-succession-contestation pattern affecting subsequent constitutional-democratic-trajectory and customary-status-administration framework.

key_developments.title: Three-kingdoms framework continuation + Catholic-monarchy framework + diaspora framework
key_developments.description: Three-kingdoms (Uvea, Sigave, Alo) framework continuation through 2023 — substantively distinctive Pacific Catholic-monarchy constitutional-recognition framework. Diaspora framework substantively significant institutional context affecting cross-border family-law adjudication with New Caledonia (substantial Wallisian/Futunan population). Substantively significant French Pacific COM institutional jurisprudential development.

key_developments.title: Cour d'appel de Nouméa + Cour de Cassation — interest-of-the-child substantive register + customary-status framework
key_developments.description: Court of Appeal of Nouméa and French Court of Cassation continue to develop interest-of-the-child jurisprudence under Civil Code arts. 371-387 framework for civil-status persons and customary-status framework for indigenous Wallisian/Futunan persons in custody disputes. Substantive analysis within French-civil-law overseas-collectivity + three-kingdoms-constitutionally-recognised framework without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label adoption.

structural_findings: Wallis and Futuna operates a structurally distinctive globally constitutionally-recognised three-kingdoms framework — only state-level entity with three constitutionally-recognised indigenous kingdoms (Uvea, Sigave, Alo) operating in parallel with French Republic civil-law framework within the corpus.
Three-kingdoms customary-status with parallel personal-status jurisdiction is structurally distinctive globally — most kingdoms-pluralist within-state framework in the corpus.
Customary courts of the three kingdoms operating under constitutional recognition is structurally distinctive within French overseas territories.
Hague Convention 1980 applicability via French Republic territorial extension.
editorial_notes: Wallis and Futuna jurisdiction sidecar v1.1 — deepened 2026-06-09 from 2 to 10 key_developments with full Wallis-protectorate-to-contemporary trajectory: 1842-French-protectorate-establishment-+-Lavelua-Amelia + 1888-Futuna-protectorate-+-Sigave-+-Alo-kingdoms-consolidation + 1946-Overseas-territory-status-+-post-WWII + 1959-Referendum-+-94-percent-in-favour-of-Territoire-d'outre-mer + 1961-Statute-Law-61-814-+-three-kingdoms-recognition + 1983-Hague-Convention-1980-territorial-extension + 1990-France-UNCRC-+-Wallis-and-Futuna-extension + 2003-Constitutional-reform-+-collectivité-d'outre-mer-Article-74 + 2018-Lavelua-succession-crisis + 2024-Cour-d'appel-de-Nouméa-+-Cour-de-Cassation-interest-of-the-child-+-customary-status.

Civil-law Pacific French overseas collectivity (Statute Law 61-814 + three constitutionally-recognised kingdoms Uvea/Sigave/Alo + customary courts + French Civil Code + Hague via French Republic territorial extension 1983). Only state-level entity with three constitutionally-recognised indigenous kingdoms globally.

PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator — substantive interest-of-the-child analysis under Civil Code arts. 371-387 for civil-status persons + Wallisian/Futunan customary-law for customary-status persons via the customary courts of the three kingdoms without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label.

Joins Pacific-Island + French-civil-law + French-overseas-collectivity-cluster + only-state-level-entity-with-three-constitutionally-recognised-indigenous-kingdoms-Uvea-Sigave-Alo-globally + Lavelua-Tu'i-Sigave-Tu'i-Agaifo-kings + most-kingdoms-pluralist-within-state-framework + Lavelua-Amelia-1842-Catholic-Marist-mission-protectorate-distinctive + Lavelua-succession-crisis-2014-2018 + 1959-Referendum-94-percent + Constitutional-reform-2003-Article-74 + customary-status-+-customary-courts-three-kingdoms + Hague-via-French-Republic-territorial-extension clusters within the corpus.
